title: External network not working correctly when internal network is present
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

When a container is connected to both external and internal networks,
external networks usually don't work (no internet connection).
(Rootless containers and networks are used in this case)

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create two networks `test_default` and `test_internal`, where `test_internal` is created with `--internal` flag:
```sh
podman network create test_default
podman network create test_internal --internal
```
2. Create a container attached to the above networks:
```sh
podman run --rm -i -t --net test_default,test_internal alpine sh
```
3. Try connecting to external IP addresses using the two interfaces

**Describe the results you received:**

Ping external IP in the container with two different interfaces:
```sh
ping -I eth0 1.1.1.1 # not working
ping -I eth1 1.1.1.1 # not working as well
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

One interface should always work for external IP addresses.


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Occasionally one interface works, but at other times neither works.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.3.0
API Version:  4.3.0
Go Version:   go1.19.2
Git Commit:   ad42af94903ce4f3c3cd0693e4e17e4286bf094b-dirty
Built:        Wed Oct 19 17:09:30 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body:
What version of Netavark do you have installed?

@Luap99 @baude We should consider adding that to `podman info`, similar to how we get info on the OCI runtime and slirp4netns
It's `netavark 1.2.0-1` from the Arch Linux package.
I think this is already fixed in in netavark (main branch, not released)
@DCsunset Could you test against the main branch
Yeah I think the main branch works without problem! I tried multiple times and the results were stable. Should I close this issue?
